facade_colour: dark mottled red-brown brick with white-framed modern glazing and white soffit trim

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: dark mottled red-brown brick with white-framed modern glazing and white soffit trim
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade has been heavily altered with modern glazing and fencing; the brickwork appears serviceable but the front yard and fence feel utilitarian.
photo_observations.window_details: Two large modern front windows dominate the ground floor, replacing the finer-grained residential opening pattern.
photo_observations.door_details: Entry is offset behind a chain-link fence and reached by concrete steps; door itself is partially obscured.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple modern eaves with minimal ornament.
photo_observations.bay_window_details: Projecting bay at second floor with 2 windows, retains original bay-and-gable form; dark mottled brick cladding on bay matches main facade
photo_observations.porch_details: Enclosed front porch with white-framed glazing and dark trim; chain-link fence in front yard; concrete steps up from sidewalk
photo_observations.chimney_details: One brick chimney is visible on the neighboring shared roofline edge.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Little historic decoration remains visible due to later recladding and storefront-like glazing changes.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Low front roof slope behind a simple parapet-like fascia
photo_observations.signage_observed: House number 35 visible near the entry.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Enclosed porch with modern glazing, replacement windows, chain-link front fence. Bay-and-gable form still intact despite alterations.
photo_observations.notes: The current facade reads as a strongly altered residential unit rather than an intact bay-and-gable house.

notes: The current facade reads as a strongly altered residential unit rather than an intact bay-and-gable house.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 1 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its converted house-form, house-form, semi-detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using fixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.

facade_description: Heavily altered converted semi-detached house, pre-1889, now reading as a single storey with cross-gable. Dark mottled red-brown brick with modern fixed windows and an enclosed front porch behind a chain-link fence. Original bay-and-gable form is still present but obscured by alterations. Party walls on both sides; 2 dwelling units. Fair condition.

Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.
